what_it_does: Records a design argument that the project has two separate annotation runtimes—board comments as addressable edges and marks as composed target state—and proposes treating an annotation envelope as a durable board item from which marks can be projected.
observations: The item claims there are two implementations of 'typed observation on an address'.
It names a board-comment runtime in runtime/cc_board.py (comment, reply, thread) and a mark runtime in runtime/suite.py and runtime/decision_registry.py (append-only mark records composing state).
It asserts the brief and design-map A5 conflate these two runtimes.
It recommends the envelope be a board item for addressability, replies, and routing, with marks as a derived read-projection.
It labels code observations as 'Verified' without presenting the evidence inline.
It includes YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, channel, part_of link, timestamps, and history.
standouts: The central claim that board-comment and mark runtimes are not the same machinery.
The proposal to keep the envelope as a board item and derive marks as read-projections.
Explicit code references to runtime/cc_board.py, runtime/suite.py, and runtime/decision_registry.py.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema (id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created, updated, history).
Uses Markdown headings and bold labels for sections.
References code modules with backtick identifiers.
Uses board:// URI addressing and a part_of link to another board item.
Breaks a neutral tone by using verdict framing ('the one verdict that matters', 'My answer').
concerns: Title is truncated in the frontmatter.
Uses 'Verified' for code claims without presenting the evidence in the file.
Frames a design decision as a verdict, which may pre-empt alternatives not documented here.
Assumes the reader knows what 'the brief', 'design-map A5', and 'the envelope' refer to.
notes: A forceful design note that splits two existing annotation mechanisms and argues for a board-item envelope with projected marks.
questions: What is the 'brief' and 'design-map A5' being referenced?
What exactly is the 'annotation envelope' in this context?
How would a mark be mechanically projected from a board item?
What evidence supports the 'Verified' code claims?
What is the 'phone artefact' mentioned as using board-comment-as-edge?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is presented as a design verdict distinguishing two runtimes and recommending a model, and it does exactly that.
apparent_themes: Addressability and threading versus composed state
intention_signals: To correct what the author sees as a category error in the design brief and establish that the annotation envelope should live in the board-comment runtime while marks remain a separate state projection.
novelty: The framing that marks are not a competing annotation runtime to board comments but a read-projection that can be derived from board items.
contribution: The distinction between addressable, threaded board items and non-addressed, append-only mark records, plus the idea of deriving composed-state marks from durable board items rather than treating them as the same thing.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item arguing that board-comment-as-edge and mark-as-composed-state are two distinct annotation runtimes, and proposing that an annotation envelope be a durable board item with marks projected from it.
intention_for_embedding: To correct a design brief's conflation of annotation runtimes and establish a hybrid model where the envelope is addressable and threadable while marks are derived state projections.
